1.G.A.1: Focuses on identifying defining attributes (like sides and closure) to classify shapes, not appearance or size.
1.G.A.2: Involves combining shapes to form new composite shapes, emphasizing composition and spatial reasoning.
1.G.A.3: Centers on partitioning shapes into equal parts and understanding fractional wholes using terms like halves and fourths.
1.MD.A.1: Order or indirectly compare lengths using a third object as a reference, without counting units.
1.MD.A.2: Measure length by counting same-size units laid end to end with no gaps or overlaps.
1.NBT.B.2: Understand that two-digit numbers are built from tens and ones, including special cases like teens and multiples of ten.
1.NBT.B.3: Compare two-digit numbers using place value and record relationships with >, =, or < symbols.
1.NBT.C.4: Add two-digit and one-digit or multiple-of-10 numbers by combining tens and ones, sometimes composing a new ten.
1.NBT.C.5: Mentally adjust a two-digit number by exactly 10 more or 10 less without counting, focusing on place value shifts.
1.NBT.C.6: Subtract multiples of 10 from other multiples of 10, working only with tens and no ones.
1.OA.A.1: Solves word problems with unknowns in all positions using addition/subtraction across five types of situations, including comparisons.
1.OA.A.2: Focuses exclusively on adding three whole numbers with a sum ≤20, emphasizing multi-addend addition over problem-type variety.
1.OA.B.3: Use properties like commutativity and associativity to rearrange numbers and simplify addition/subtraction strategies.
1.OA.B.4: Solve subtraction by finding the missing addend that combines with the subtrahend to reach the minuend.
1.OA.C.5: Relate counting directly to addition and subtraction by using counting on or back as the method itself.
1.OA.C.6: Use multiple strategic methods (making ten, decomposing, etc.) to achieve fluency in adding and subtracting within 20.
1.OA.D.7: Evaluate if equations are true or false by understanding the equal sign as balance, not just “the answer comes next.”
1.OA.D.8: Find the missing number in an equation to make it true, focusing on solving for an unknown quantity.
2.G.A.1: Identify and draw shapes by counting specified angles or faces, not by measuring size.
2.G.A.2: Partition rectangles into same-size squares using rows and columns to count total area units.
2.G.A.3: Divide circles/rectangles into equal shares using fractional words, allowing different shapes for equal parts.
2.MD.A.1: Use appropriate tools (rulers, tapes, etc.) to physically measure an object’s length with precision.
2.MD.A.2: Measure the same object twice with different-sized units and explain how unit size affects the measurement number.
2.MD.A.3: Approximate an object’s length without tools using familiar units like inches or centimeters.
2.MD.A.4: Find the difference in length between two objects and express it as a standard unit value.
2.MD.B.5: Solve word problems involving lengths using drawings and equations to find unknown values through addition or subtraction.
2.MD.B.6: Represent sums and differences on a number line by showing jumps between whole-number points as visual models.
2.MD.C.7: Tell and write time using analog/digital clocks to the nearest five minutes with a.m./p.m. notation.
2.MD.C.8: Solve word problems involving money amounts using $ and ¢ symbols with coins and bills.
2.MD.D.10: Draw picture and bar graphs to represent categorical data and solve problems using comparisons between distinct categories.
2.MD.D.9: Generate and display measurement data as a line plot with whole-number units to show variation in continuous length measurements.
2.NBT.A.1: Understand place value by decomposing three-digit numbers into hundreds, tens, and ones, emphasizing bundled units like 100 as ten tens.
2.NBT.A.2: Skip-count by 5s, 10s, and 100s to build fluency in number patterns, not value decomposition.
2.NBT.A.3: Read and write numbers in three forms—numerals, names, expanded—focusing on representation, not magnitude or structure.
2.NBT.A.4: Compare three-digit numbers using >, =, < based on digit values, emphasizing relational reasoning over representation or counting.
2.NBT.B.5: Focuses on fluently adding/subtracting two-digit numbers within 100 using place value and properties, without requiring concrete models.
2.NBT.B.6: Specifically requires adding up to four two-digit numbers, emphasizing multiple addends beyond two.
2.NBT.B.7: Involves three-digit numbers and requires relating strategies to written methods, including composing/decomposing hundreds.
2.NBT.B.8: Centers on mental math for adding/subtracting only 10 or 100, not general computation.
2.NBT.B.9: Demands explaining why strategies work using place value and properties, focusing on reasoning over computation.
2.OA.C.3: Focuses on identifying odd/even numbers by pairing or skip-counting, then expressing even numbers as two equal addends.
2.OA.C.4: Uses addition to find totals in rectangular arrays by summing equal rows or columns, emphasizing structure over parity.
3.G.A.1: Identify how shapes share attributes to define broader categories like quadrilaterals, distinguishing by classification based on side and angle properties.
3.G.A.2: Divide shapes into equal-area parts and express each part as a unit fraction, distinguishing by focusing on area partitioning and fractional representation.
3.MD.A.1: Focuses on measuring and calculating time intervals in minutes using number lines, distinct from volume/mass measurements.
3.MD.A.2: Involves measuring and operating on liquid volumes and masses in grams, kilograms, or liters, unlike time-based calculations.
3.MD.B.3: Draw scaled graphs to compare categorical data and solve “how many more/less” problems using bars representing groups.
3.MD.B.4: Generate and plot continuous measurement data on a line plot with fractional units like halves and quarters.
3.MD.C.5: Recognize area as a measurable attribute of plane figures, distinct from length or perimeter.
3.MD.C.6: Measure area by physically counting unit squares, emphasizing direct enumeration over calculation.
3.MD.C.7: Relate area to multiplication and addition, using formulas and decomposing shapes to compute area.
3.NBT.A.1: Round whole numbers to nearest 10 or 100 using place value to estimate, not calculate exact sums or products.
3.NBT.A.2: Fluently add and subtract within 1000 using place value and operations, focusing on exact computation, not estimation or multiplication.
3.NBT.A.3: Multiply one-digit numbers by multiples of 10 using place value, uniquely focusing on multiplication with tens, not addition/subtraction or rounding.
3.NF.A.1: Defines fractions as parts of a whole using partitioning and counting equal-sized portions.
3.NF.A.2: Represents fractions as precise points on a number line, emphasizing location and magnitude.
3.NF.A.3: Focuses on identifying equivalent fractions and comparing sizes through reasoning, not just representation.
3.OA.A.1: Focuses on interpreting multiplication as total objects in equal-sized groups, emphasizing grouping structure over division or unknowns.
3.OA.A.2: Distinguishes by interpreting division as either equal shares or number of groups, highlighting partitioning contexts.
3.OA.A.3: Centers on solving real-world word problems using multiplication/division across contexts like arrays and measurement, not just computation.
3.OA.A.4: Unique in finding missing numbers in equations, prioritizing algebraic reasoning over contextual interpretation or problem-solving.
3.OA.B.5: Use properties like commutative, associative, and distributive to rewrite or break down multiplication and division expressions differently.
3.OA.B.6: Solve division by finding the missing factor in a related multiplication equation.
3.OA.D.8: Solve multi-step word problems using operations and equations with unknowns, then evaluate answer reasonableness through estimation.
3.OA.D.9: Identify and explain numerical patterns using properties of operations, focusing on structural relationships, not solving for unknowns.
4.G.A.1: Draw and identify geometric elements like points, lines, and angles as foundational building blocks of shapes.
4.G.A.2: Classify shapes by analyzing presence of parallel/perpendicular lines or specific angle types, especially right triangles.
4.G.A.3: Identify and draw lines of symmetry where a figure folds perfectly onto itself in matching halves.
4.MD.A.1: Convert between units within a system by expressing larger units as multiples of smaller ones and recording equivalents in tables.
4.MD.A.2: Solve real-world word problems using operations with measurements, including fractions/decimals, and represent them on number line diagrams.
4.MD.A.3: Apply area and perimeter formulas for rectangles to find unknown dimensions using multiplication and division equations.
4.MD.C.5: Define angles as geometric shapes measured by fractional parts of a circle, establishing the concept of a degree as 1/360 of a full rotation.
4.MD.C.6: Use a protractor to physically measure or draw angles in whole-number degrees, focusing on tool-based execution.
4.MD.C.7: Treat angle measures as additive quantities that can be combined or split to solve for unknowns using equations.
4.NBT.A.1: Recognize that a digit’s value is ten times greater than the digit to its right, emphasizing multiplicative place value relationships.
4.NBT.A.2: Read, write, and compare multi-digit numbers using numerals, names, and expanded form, focusing on digit-by-digit positional meaning.
4.NBT.A.3: Round numbers to any place based on place value, emphasizing estimation and proximity to nearest benchmark rather than exact value.
4.NBT.B.4: Uses the standard algorithm exclusively for fluent addition and subtraction of large whole numbers.
4.NBT.B.5: Multiplies multi-digit by one-digit or two-digit numbers using place value strategies and visual models.
4.NBT.B.6: Finds quotients and remainders with division, leveraging place value and multiplication relationships.
4.NF.A.1: Explain how multiplying numerator and denominator by the same number creates equivalent fractions with different-sized parts but same value.
4.NF.A.2: Compare two different fractions using common denominators, numerators, or benchmarks to determine which is larger, smaller, or equal.
4.NF.B.3: Understand a fraction as a sum of unit fractions, emphasizing addition and decomposition.
4.NF.B.4: Multiply a fraction by a whole number, focusing on repeated addition as multiplication.
4.NF.C.5: Convert tenths to hundredths to add fractions with denominators 10 and 100 using equivalent fractions.
4.NF.C.6: Rewrite fractions with denominators 10 or 100 as decimal notation and apply to real-world measurements.
4.NF.C.7: Compare two decimals to hundredths using symbols >, =, <, ensuring they refer to the same whole.
4.OA.A.1: Focuses on interpreting multiplication equations as statements of multiplicative comparison, not solving word problems.
4.OA.A.2: Distinguishes multiplicative from additive comparisons by solving word problems using multiplication or division.
4.OA.A.3: Solves multistep word problems using all four operations and assesses reasonableness with estimation and rounding.
5.G.A.1: Define a coordinate system using perpendicular axes and ordered pairs to locate points by interpreting x- and y-coordinate directions from the origin.
5.G.A.2: Graph and interpret points in the first quadrant to solve real-world and mathematical problems, focusing on contextual meaning of coordinates.
5.G.B.3: Attributes of a category automatically apply to all its subcategories, emphasizing inheritance of properties.
5.G.B.4: Organize shapes into a hierarchical structure based on shared geometric properties, focusing on classification order.
5.MD.C.3: Recognize volume as a measurable attribute of solid figures, distinct from length or area.
5.MD.C.4: Measure volume by physically counting unit cubes using standard or improvised units.
5.MD.C.5: Calculate volume using multiplication and addition operations to solve real-world problems.
5.NBT.A.1: A digit’s value changes by factors of 10 based on its position relative to adjacent places.
5.NBT.A.2: Powers of 10 shift digits via multiplication/division, altering zeros or decimal point placement.
5.NBT.A.3: Decimals to thousandths are expressed and compared using place value notation and symbols.
5.NBT.A.4: Decimals are rounded to specified places by evaluating the next digit’s value for approximation.
5.NBT.B.5: Fluently multiply multi-digit whole numbers using the standard algorithm—no models or explanations required, just procedural fluency.
5.NBT.B.6: Find whole-number quotients using place value strategies and visual models, emphasizing reasoning over rote algorithm use.
5.NBT.B.7: Operate on decimals to hundredths using concrete representations and explain reasoning, distinguishing it by decimal focus and conceptual justification.
5.NF.A.1: Focuses on the procedural skill of finding equivalent fractions to add or subtract with unlike denominators using mathematical algorithms.
5.NF.A.2: Emphasizes solving real-world word problems and using estimation/benchmark fractions to judge reasonableness of answers.
5.NF.B.3: Focuses on interpreting fractions as division results, not multiplication or scaling—distinguishing it by equating a/b to a ÷ b.
5.NF.B.4: Centers on multiplying fractions or whole numbers by fractions, unlike division or scaling interpretations in other standards.
5.NF.B.5: Distinguishes itself by interpreting multiplication as resizing or scaling, comparing product sizes without calculating.
5.NF.B.6: Solves real-world multiplication problems with fractions/mixed numbers, differing by emphasizing application over conceptual interpretation.
5.NF.B.7: Focuses exclusively on dividing unit fractions and whole numbers, uniquely introducing division with fractions not covered elsewhere.
5.OA.A.1: Evaluate numerical expressions by applying parentheses, brackets, or braces to determine order of operations.
5.OA.A.2: Write and interpret expressions without calculating, focusing on structural relationships like multiplication as scaling.
6.EE.A.1: Focuses on evaluating expressions with whole-number exponents, distinct by its exclusive use of numerical bases and powers.
6.EE.A.2: Introduces variables (letters) into expressions, distinguishing it by representing unknown numbers symbolically.
6.EE.A.3: Involves transforming expressions using properties like distribution to create new but equivalent forms, centered on generation via operations.
6.EE.A.4: Centers on recognizing equivalence through substitution, distinguishing it by verifying identity across all possible values.
6.EE.B.5: Focuses on testing specific values via substitution to determine if they satisfy an equation or inequality.
6.EE.B.6: Emphasizes using variables to represent unknowns or any number in a set within expressions.
6.EE.B.7: Centers on solving one-step equations with nonnegative rational numbers to find a single solution.
6.EE.B.8: Highlights writing and graphing inequalities with infinitely many solutions on a number line.
6.G.A.1: Focuses on calculating area by composing or decomposing 2D shapes into rectangles and triangles.
6.G.A.2: Centers on finding volume of prisms with fractional sides using unit cubes or formulas.
6.G.A.3: Uses coordinate plane to draw polygons and calculate side lengths via point coordinates.
6.G.A.4: Involves unfolding 3D figures into 2D nets to compute surface area.
6.NS.B.2: Focuses solely on dividing multi-digit whole numbers using the standard division algorithm.
6.NS.B.3: Involves all four operations with multi-digit decimals, emphasizing decimal placement in algorithms.
6.NS.B.4: Centers on GCF, LCM, and applying the distributive property to rewrite sums with common factors.
6.NS.C.5: Use positive and negative numbers to represent real-world quantities with opposite directions and explain the meaning of zero in context.
6.NS.C.6: Represent rational numbers as points on a number line and extend coordinate axes to include negative values.
6.NS.C.7: Compare rational numbers by ordering and interpret their absolute value as distance from zero.
6.NS.C.8: Solve problems by graphing points in all four quadrants and use coordinates/absolute value to find horizontal/vertical distances.
6.RP.A.1: Describes ratio relationships using comparative language like “for every” without calculating unit values.
6.RP.A.2: Focuses on expressing a ratio as a single-unit rate, such as “per one” unit, to quantify proportionality.
6.RP.A.3: Applies ratio and rate reasoning through strategies like tables or diagrams to solve practical problems.
6.SP.A.1: Distinguishes statistical questions by anticipating variability in data answers, unlike non-statistical ones with single answers.
6.SP.A.2: Focuses on describing data distribution through center, spread, and shape, not just identifying variability.
6.SP.A.3: Contrasts summary measures—center (typical value) vs. variation (spread of values)—as distinct data summaries.
6.SP.B.4: Display numerical data using visual plots like dot plots, histograms, or box plots to show distribution shape and spread.
6.SP.B.5: Summarize data contextually by calculating center/variability measures and interpreting patterns, deviations, and measurement details.
7.EE.A.1: Use operations to add, subtract, factor, or expand linear expressions with rational coefficients—focus on algebraic manipulation techniques.
7.EE.A.2: Interpret rewritten expressions to reveal contextual relationships, like percent change as multiplication—focus on meaning, not just form.
7.EE.B.3: Solve multi-step problems with rational numbers in any form, emphasizing estimation, conversion, and reasonableness checks without variables.
7.EE.B.4: Use variables to represent unknowns and construct equations or inequalities to solve problems through algebraic reasoning.
7.G.A.1: Focuses on scaling drawings proportionally to compute or reproduce actual sizes using scale factors.
7.G.A.2: Centers on constructing triangles from given angle/side measures to determine if they form unique, multiple, or no triangles.
7.G.A.3: Involves visualizing and describing 2D cross-sections resulting from slicing 3D figures like prisms and pyramids.
7.G.B.4: Focuses on circle-specific formulas and the relationship between circumference and area, not other shapes.
7.G.B.5: Centers on writing/solving equations for unknown angles using geometric angle relationships.
7.G.B.6: Involves area, volume, and surface area of composite 2D and 3D figures beyond circles and angles.
7.NS.A.1: Focuses on adding and subtracting rational numbers using number line representations to visualize direction and distance.
7.NS.A.2: Emphasizes multiplying and dividing rational numbers by extending fraction rules to negative and positive values.
7.NS.A.3: Requires solving real-world problems using all four operations with rational numbers, integrating fluency and context.
7.RP.A.1: Compute unit rates by dividing two fractions or quantities in different units to find a per-unit value.
7.RP.A.2: Identify and represent proportional relationships using tables, graphs, or equations to show constant ratios.
7.RP.A.3: Apply proportional reasoning to solve real-world multistep problems involving percents and financial contexts.
7.SP.A.1: Focuses on why representative sampling and randomness are necessary for valid population generalizations, not on generating or analyzing multiple samples.
7.SP.A.2: Emphasizes using multiple random samples to measure variation in estimates and quantify prediction uncertainty.
7.SP.B.3: Focuses on visually comparing two distributions by expressing center differences as multiples of variability, emphasizing visual overlap on dot plots.
7.SP.B.4: Uses sample measures to make informal comparative inferences about entire populations, not just visual overlap.
7.SP.C.5: Defines probability as a numerical likelihood between 0 and 1, distinguishing it by conceptual understanding rather than measurement or modeling.
7.SP.C.6: Uses observed relative frequency from data collection to approximate probability, distinguishing it by empirical, real-world experimentation.
7.SP.C.7: Creates a theoretical probability model to calculate event probabilities, distinguishing it by constructing a framework before comparing to data.
7.SP.C.8: Calculates probabilities of compound events using tools like tree diagrams or simulations, distinguishing it by handling multiple interconnected outcomes.
8.EE.A.1: Apply exponent properties to simplify and generate equivalent numerical expressions with integer exponents.
8.EE.A.2: Use root symbols to solve and represent solutions for equations involving squares and cubes of variables.
8.EE.A.3: Estimate and compare magnitudes using single-digit times powers of ten to express relative size.
8.EE.A.4: Perform arithmetic operations on numbers in scientific notation and interpret technology-generated notation.
8.EE.B.5: Graph and compare proportional relationships using unit rate as slope, focusing on real-world comparisons across different representations.
8.EE.B.6: Use similar triangles to prove slope consistency and derive linear equations, emphasizing geometric reasoning behind y = mx + b.
8.EE.C.7: Solve single linear equations by isolating one variable using inverse operations.
8.EE.C.8: Find intersection points of two linear equations by solving them together as a system.
8.F.A.1: A function assigns exactly one output to each input, defining the core relationship between inputs and outputs.
8.F.A.2: Compare functions across different representations to determine which has a greater rate of change or other properties.
8.F.A.3: Identify linear functions by their straight-line graphs and distinguish them from non-linear functions like quadratics.
8.F.B.4: Construct a linear function and calculate its exact rate of change and initial value from data or context.
8.F.B.5: Describe or sketch a function’s qualitative behavior—like increasing/decreasing or linear/nonlinear—without numerical values.
8.G.A.1: Experimentally verify that rotations, reflections, and translations preserve lengths, angle measures, and parallelism.
8.G.A.2: Define congruence as exact matching via rigid motions without size change.
8.G.A.3: Use coordinates to precisely describe how transformations move figures on the plane.
8.G.A.4: Define similarity as matching shape via rigid motions plus dilations that scale size.
8.G.A.5: Use informal reasoning and angle relationships to prove geometric facts about triangles and transversals.
8.G.B.6: Explain the proof and converse of the Pythagorean Theorem, focusing on logical reasoning rather than calculation.
8.G.B.7: Apply the theorem to find missing side lengths in right triangles within real-world and 2D/3D mathematical problems.
8.G.B.8: Use the theorem to calculate distance between two coordinate points by treating the line segment as a hypotenuse.
8.NS.A.1: Identify irrational numbers by their non-repeating decimal expansions and convert repeating decimals into fractions.
8.NS.A.2: Approximate and locate irrational numbers on a number line using rational truncations to estimate values.
8.SP.A.1: Construct and interpret scatter plots to identify visual patterns like clustering, outliers, and linear/nonlinear associations between two quantitative variables.
8.SP.A.2: Informally fit a straight line to scatter plots suggesting linear association and judge model fit by data point closeness to the line.
8.SP.A.3: Use the equation of a fitted linear model to solve problems by interpreting slope and intercept as real-world rates and starting values.
8.SP.A.4: Analyze bivariate categorical data using two-way tables and relative frequencies to detect associations between non-numeric variables.
A-APR.B.2: Uses the Remainder Theorem to link polynomial evaluation at a to divisibility by (x – a), focusing on remainder and factor relationships.
A-APR.B.3: Uses known zeros from factorizations to sketch polynomial graphs, emphasizing visual representation over algebraic proof.
A-APR.C.4: Prove polynomial identities to reveal numerical patterns, like generating Pythagorean triples through algebraic equivalence.
A-APR.C.5: Apply the Binomial Theorem to expand (x+y)^n using combinatorial coefficients, distinct in its focus on power expansion and Pascal’s Triangle.
A-APR.D.6: Rewrite rational expressions using division to express them as a polynomial plus a remainder over the divisor.
A-APR.D.7: Perform arithmetic operations on rational expressions, emphasizing their closure under those operations like rational numbers.
A-CED.A.1: Create one-variable equations/inequalities to solve for a single unknown quantity using various function types.
A-CED.A.2: Create multi-variable equations and graph them to show relationships between two or more changing quantities.
A-CED.A.3: Use equations/inequalities to model real-world constraints and judge whether solutions make sense in context.
A-CED.A.4: Rearrange formulas algebraically to isolate and solve for a specific variable of interest.
A-REI.A.1: Justify each step in solving an equation by logically explaining how equality is preserved, emphasizing reasoning over computation.
A-REI.A.2: Solve rational and radical equations and identify extraneous solutions that emerge from squaring or multiplying both sides.
A-REI.B.3: Solve linear equations and inequalities in one variable, including those with letter coefficients, emphasizing linear relationships and symbolic manipulation.
A-REI.B.4: Solve quadratic equations in one variable, focusing on non-linear solutions via factoring, completing the square, or the quadratic formula.
A-REI.C.5: Proves that adding a multiple of one equation to another preserves solutions, emphasizing equation manipulation over solving.
A-REI.C.6: Solves systems of two linear equations exactly or approximately, focusing on solution methods like graphing or substitution.
A-REI.C.7: Solves mixed linear-quadratic systems, distinguishing itself by combining different function types.
A-REI.C.8: Represents linear systems as single matrix equations, introducing matrix notation as a structural tool.
A-REI.C.9: Uses matrix inverses to solve systems, uniquely requiring inverse computation and technology for larger matrices.
A-REI.D.10: The graph represents all solution pairs of an equation as points forming a curve or line in the coordinate plane.
A-REI.D.11: Solutions arise from the x-coordinates where two function graphs intersect, equating f(x) and g(x).
A-REI.D.12: Solutions are graphed as shaded half-planes or their intersection, representing inequality regions, not just curves.
A-SSE.A.1: Interpret expressions by connecting their parts to real-world quantities and context.
A-SSE.A.2: Use structural patterns (like difference of squares) to rewrite expressions algebraically.
A-SSE.B.3: Transform expressions to uncover hidden properties like zeros, maxima, or symmetry through factoring or completing the square.
A-SSE.B.4: Derive and apply a specific formula for summing finite geometric sequences, distinct in its structured progression and real-world application like mortgages.
F-BF.A.1: Write a function to model a general relationship between two quantities using any form—no restriction to sequences.
F-BF.A.2: Write and convert between recursive and explicit formulas specifically for arithmetic and geometric sequences.
F-BF.B.3: Transform graphs via shifts, stretches, or reflections by modifying inputs/outputs with constant k, distinguishing it by geometric manipulation of functions.
F-BF.B.4: Solve for the inverse function by reversing input-output pairs, uniquely focused on function reversal, not transformation.
F-BF.B.5: Leverage the inverse link between exponents and logs to convert and solve equations, uniquely tied to logarithmic-exponential relationships.
F-IF.A.1: Defines a function as a unique assignment from domain to range, establishing the core concept of one-output-per-input.
F-IF.A.2: Focuses on applying and interpreting function notation like f(x) within real-world contexts, not just defining functions.
F-IF.A.3: Identifies sequences as functions with integer domains, often defined recursively, distinguishing them from continuous functions.
F-IF.B.4: Interpret visual and tabular features like intercepts, maxima, and end behavior to understand overall function behavior across its domain.
F-IF.B.5: Connect the function’s domain to realistic input values based on context, not just graphical shape.
F-IF.B.6: Compute and interpret the slope between two points as an average rate of change over a specific interval.
F-IF.C.7: Graph functions symbolically to visually display key features like intercepts and maxima using hand or technology.
F-IF.C.8: Rewrite function expressions into equivalent forms to uncover hidden properties such as vertex or zeros.
F-IF.C.9: Compare two functions across different representations to determine which has greater or lesser properties like rate of change or maxima.
F-LE.A.1: Identify whether a situation requires a linear or exponential model based on constant difference versus constant ratio.
F-LE.A.2: Build linear or exponential functions from data points, graphs, or descriptions using explicit formulas or recursive patterns.
F-LE.A.3: Compare long-term growth rates to show exponential functions outpace linear, quadratic, or polynomial functions.
F-LE.A.4: Solve exponential equations by rewriting them as logarithms and using technology to compute the solution.
F-TF.A.1: Radian measure is defined as the arc length on the unit circle, distinguishing it by linking angle measure directly to distance.
F-TF.A.2: Extends trig functions to all real numbers via radian-based circular traversal, emphasizing domain expansion beyond acute angles.
F-TF.A.3: Uses special triangles and unit circle reflections to compute exact trig values for specific angles and their symmetries.
F-TF.A.4: Focuses on symmetry (odd/even) and periodicity properties of trig functions using unit circle patterns.
F-TF.B.5: Model periodic phenomena by selecting trig functions with specific amplitude, frequency, and midline—focuses on fitting real-world cycles.
F-TF.B.6: Construct inverse trig functions by restricting domains to ensure monotonicity—focuses on enabling invertibility through domain control.
F-TF.B.7: Solve trig equations using inverses and interpret results contextually—focuses on applying inverses to find and analyze solutions.
F-TF.C.8: Uses the Pythagorean identity to relate sine and cosine values and find missing trig functions based on quadrant information.
F-TF.C.9: Applies addition and subtraction formulas to compute trig values of combined or differing angles, not just single-angle relationships.
G-C.A.1: Prove similarity by showing all circles scale uniformly, regardless of size—unlike others, it’s about global shape equivalence, not local relationships.
G-C.A.2: Identify angle and segment relationships within a circle, focusing on measures and perpendicularity, unlike construction-based standards.
G-C.A.3: Construct circles around or inside triangles and use them to prove quadrilateral angle properties—unique in combining construction with proof for polygons.
G-C.A.4: Construct a tangent from an external point—distinct for requiring geometric construction to a specific external condition not covered by other standards.
G-CO.A.1: Defines foundational geometric terms using undefined notions, focusing on precise vocabulary, not transformations.
G-CO.A.2: Treats transformations as functions mapping points, distinguishing by comparing preservation of distance and angle.
G-CO.A.3: Identifies symmetries (rotations/reflections) that map specific shapes onto themselves, emphasizing self-mapping.
G-CO.A.4: Builds definitions of transformations using geometric elements like angles and circles, not just applying them.
G-CO.A.5: Requires drawing and sequencing transformations to map one figure to another, emphasizing execution and order.
G-CO.B.6: Use rigid motions to transform figures and determine congruence by moving one figure onto another.
G-CO.B.7: Prove triangle congruence by matching all corresponding sides and angles through rigid motions.
G-CO.B.8: Derive ASA, SAS, and SSS criteria as shortcuts to congruence based on rigid motion properties.
G-CO.C.9: Proves properties of lines and angles, focusing on transversals, vertical angles, and perpendicular bisectors as equidistant loci.
G-CO.C.10: Proves triangle-specific theorems centered on angle sums, midsegments, and medians unique to triangular structures.
G-CO.C.11: Proves parallelogram properties centered on opposite sides/angles and diagonal bisection, distinguishing it via quadrilateral symmetry.
G-CO.D.12: Construct basic geometric elements like segments, angles, bisectors, and parallels using various tools, focusing on foundational constructions.
G-CO.D.13: Inscribe specific regular polygons (triangle, square, hexagon) precisely within a circle, emphasizing cyclic symmetry and circle-based constraints.
G-GMD.A.1: Justify circle and cone/cylinder/pyramid formulas using dissection, Cavalieri’s, and limit arguments—focuses on deriving formulas, not applying them.
G-GMD.A.2: Use Cavalieri’s principle specifically to argue sphere volume—distinct for targeting spheres and complex solids with cross-sections.
G-GMD.A.3: Apply known volume formulas to solve real-world problems—differs by emphasizing computation, not derivation or justification.
G-GPE.A.1: Uses the Pythagorean Theorem to derive a circle’s equation from center and radius, focusing on constant distance from a single point.
G-GPE.A.2: Derives a parabola’s equation using equidistance from a focus and a directrix line, not a center point.
G-GPE.A.3: Derives ellipses/hyperbolas using sum or difference of distances from two foci, distinguishing them by two focal points.
G-GPE.B.4: Use coordinates to algebraically prove or disprove geometric properties like rectangle shape or point-on-circle membership.
G-GPE.B.5: Prove and apply slope criteria to determine parallelism or perpendicularity between lines.
G-GPE.B.6: Locate a specific point along a line segment that divides it in a given ratio.
G-GPE.B.7: Calculate perimeters and areas using coordinate distances, not proving relationships.
G-MG.A.1: Use geometric shapes to describe real-world objects by identifying their form and properties.
G-MG.A.2: Apply density concepts using area or volume ratios to model quantitative physical relationships.
G-MG.A.3: Solve design problems using geometric methods to meet constraints or optimize cost and function.
G-SRT.A.1: Dilations transform figures by scaling distances from a center, altering lengths proportionally while preserving parallelism and fixed lines through the center.
G-SRT.A.2: Similarity is determined by checking if one figure can be mapped to another via transformations that preserve angles and scale sides proportionally.
G-SRT.A.3: AA similarity is established by showing two pairs of equal angles imply similarity, without needing side measurements or full transformations.
G-SRT.B.4: Prove triangle theorems using similarity, specifically parallel lines creating proportional segments and deriving the Pythagorean Theorem via similarity.
G-SRT.B.5: Apply congruence and similarity criteria to solve problems and prove relationships, emphasizing practical use over proof derivation.
G-SRT.C.6: Defines trigonometric ratios as consistent side ratios in right triangles determined solely by acute angle measures.
G-SRT.C.7: Highlights the sine-cosine relationship between complementary angles, not individual ratios or applications.
G-SRT.C.8: Applies trig ratios and Pythagorean Theorem to solve real-world right triangle problems, not just define or relate ratios.
G-SRT.D.9: Derive the area formula using an auxiliary perpendicular line, focusing on geometric construction rather than solving for sides or angles.
G-SRT.D.10: Prove the Laws of Sines and Cosines through logical reasoning, emphasizing derivation over application.
G-SRT.D.11: Apply the Laws of Sines and Cosines to solve real-world problems involving unknown measurements in any triangle.
K.CC.A.1: Count sequentially to 100 using ones and tens as counting patterns.
K.CC.A.2: Start counting from any given number, not just from 1, within the number sequence.
K.CC.A.3: Write numerals and match them to quantities, focusing on symbol-object correspondence.
K.CC.B.4: Understand that the last number said tells the total quantity, linking counting to cardinality.
K.CC.B.5: Physically count objects in various arrangements to answer “how many?” up to 20.
K.CC.C.6: Compare quantities of objects using matching or counting to determine greater than, less than, or equal to.
K.CC.C.7: Compare written numerals (1–10) directly without using physical objects.
K.G.A.1: Describe object positions using spatial terms like above or beside to show relationships between shapes in the environment.
K.G.A.2: Correctly name shapes regardless of orientation or size, focusing on identity over appearance.
K.G.A.3: Identify shapes as flat (2D) or solid (3D) based on their dimensional nature.
K.G.B.4: Analyze and compare shapes by identifying and describing their parts and attributes using informal language.
K.G.B.5: Model shapes by physically building or drawing them to represent real-world objects.
K.G.B.6: Compose larger shapes by combining simpler shapes with full sides touching.
K.MD.A.1: Identify and name multiple measurable attributes (like length, weight) of a single object.
K.MD.A.2: Directly compare two objects using a shared measurable attribute to determine which has more or less.
K.OA.A.1: Represent addition/subtraction using diverse methods like objects, drawings, or sounds to show the process visually or physically.
K.OA.A.2: Solve word problems by applying addition/subtraction within 10 using concrete or visual representations to find answers.
K.OA.A.3: Decompose numbers into multiple unique pairs to explore different ways a number can be broken apart.
K.OA.A.4: Find the missing addend that combines with a given number to make exactly 10, focusing on complements to ten.
K.OA.A.5: Achieve quick, accurate addition and subtraction within 5 without relying on objects or drawings.
N-CN.A.1: Defines the imaginary unit i and the structural form a + bi for all complex numbers.
N-CN.A.2: Applies algebraic properties to perform arithmetic operations on complex numbers.
N-CN.A.3: Uses conjugates to compute moduli and divide complex numbers, a unique operational tool.
N-CN.B.4: Represent complex numbers in both rectangular and polar forms to show they are equivalent representations of the same number.
N-CN.B.5: Use geometric operations on the complex plane to compute complex arithmetic via modulus and argument properties.
N-CN.B.6: Find distance and midpoint between complex numbers using modulus and averaging, not transformation or representation.
N-CN.C.7: Solve quadratic equations with real coefficients that have complex solutions.
N-CN.C.8: Extend polynomial identities to the complex numbers by factoring sums of squares using imaginary units.
N-CN.C.9: Know the Fundamental Theorem of Algebra; show that it is true for quadratic polynomials.
N-Q.A.1: Use units and scales to interpret and guide problem-solving, ensuring consistency across formulas and visual representations.
N-Q.A.2: Define meaningful quantities to create realistic descriptive models for real-world contexts.
N-Q.A.3: Select appropriate measurement accuracy based on inherent limitations in data or tools.
N-RN.A.1: Explains why rational exponents are defined as radicals by extending integer exponent properties to maintain consistency.
N-RN.A.2: Rewrites expressions by applying exponent rules to convert between radicals and rational exponents.
N-VM.A.1: Define vectors by their inherent magnitude and direction, represented as directed segments with proper notation.
N-VM.A.2: Calculate vector components by subtracting initial from terminal coordinates to determine horizontal and vertical parts.
N-VM.A.3: Apply vectors to solve real-world problems involving velocity and other directional quantities.
N-VM.B.4: Add and subtract vectors by combining their magnitudes and directions component-wise or graphically.
N-VM.B.5: Multiply a vector by a scalar to scale its magnitude and possibly reverse its direction.
N-VM.C.10: Focuses on the roles of zero/identity matrices and determinants determining invertibility, unlike other standards that focus on operations or transformations.
N-VM.C.11: Distinguishes by multiplying matrices with vectors to produce new vectors, emphasizing vector transformation over general matrix operations.
N-VM.C.12: Unique in interpreting 2×2 matrices as plane transformations and linking determinant’s absolute value to area scaling.
N-VM.C.6: Centers on using matrices as data representations (e.g., networks or payoffs), not operations or transformations.
N-VM.C.7: Specifically about scalar multiplication of matrices, distinct from matrix-matrix operations or transformations.
N-VM.C.8: Focuses on basic arithmetic operations (add, subtract, multiply) between matrices of compatible dimensions.
N-VM.C.9: Highlights non-commutativity of matrix multiplication while affirming associativity/distributivity, unlike other standards on structure or use.
S-CP.A.1: Events are described as subsets using set operations like unions, intersections, or complements to combine or exclude outcomes.
S-CP.A.2: Independence is determined by whether the joint probability equals the product of individual probabilities.
S-CP.A.3: Conditional probability defines P(A|B) as P(A and B)/P(B), linking it directly to independence via equality with P(A).
S-CP.A.4: Two-way tables organize categorical data to empirically estimate conditional probabilities and test independence from observed frequencies.
S-CP.A.5: Conditional probability and independence are interpreted in real-world contexts by reversing conditions, like cancer vs. smoking likelihoods.
S-CP.B.6: Computes probability of A given B by focusing on the subset of B outcomes that include A, emphasizing dependency.
S-CP.B.7: Calculates probability of A or B by adding individual probabilities and subtracting their overlap to avoid double-counting.
S-CP.B.8: Uses conditional probability to express joint probability through multiplication, linking A and B via dependence.
S-CP.B.9: Counts compound event outcomes using permutations or combinations to determine theoretical probabilities in ordered or unordered sets.
S-IC.A.1: Statistics infer population parameters from random samples, focusing on estimation from data.
S-IC.A.2: Evaluate model consistency with observed data using simulation, focusing on hypothesis testing against expected outcomes.
S-IC.B.3: Distinguishes among survey, experiment, and observational study types by their purpose and how randomization applies to each.
S-IC.B.4: Estimates population parameters from sample surveys and uses simulation to calculate margin of error for sampling variability.
S-IC.B.5: Compares treatment effects in randomized experiments using simulations to assess statistical significance of differences.
S-IC.B.6: Critiques the validity and reasoning of published data reports, focusing on methodology and conclusions.
S-ID.A.1: Represent data visually on a number line using dot plots, histograms, or box plots—focusing on graphical display, not analysis.
S-ID.A.2: Compare center and spread using appropriate statistics to quantify differences between data sets, not interpret context.
S-ID.A.3: Interpret shape, center, and spread differences in real-world context, explicitly accounting for outliers’ effects.
S-ID.A.4: Fit data to a normal distribution to estimate population percentages using mean and standard deviation—unique in assuming normality.
S-ID.B.5: Summarize and interpret categorical data using two-way tables and relative frequencies to identify associations between categories.
S-ID.B.6: Represent and describe relationships between two quantitative variables using scatter plots and patterns like correlation or trend.
S-ID.C.7: Interpret the slope and intercept of a linear model to understand how one variable changes with another in context.
S-ID.C.8: Compute and interpret the correlation coefficient to measure the strength and direction of a linear relationship.
S-ID.C.9: Distinguish correlation from causation to avoid falsely inferring cause from association alone.
S-MD.A.1: Define a random variable by assigning numerical values to sample space events and graphing its probability distribution like data distributions.
S-MD.A.2: Calculate and interpret the expected value as the mean of a probability distribution, regardless of how it’s derived.
S-MD.A.3: Develop theoretical probability distributions using mathematical models to compute expected values.
S-MD.A.4: Construct empirical probability distributions from real-world data to calculate expected values.
S-MD.B.5: Calculate expected values by weighting payoffs with probabilities to quantify potential outcomes.
S-MD.B.6: Use probability to ensure fairness in decisions through randomization methods like lots or generators.
S-MD.B.7: Apply probability to evaluate real-world strategies and make informed decisions under uncertainty.
